Plan/dag-agent-dispatched against a seed node.\\nwrite_domain: \\\"blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex\\\"\\nread_only: false\\ncan_spawn: true\\ndefault_enabled: false\\ndispatcher_notes: |\\n  - Dispatch me against a SEED declaration whose foundation you want made\\n    complete: an \\u221e-source (`archon dag-query gaps`), the project goal, or a\\n    frontier node you suspect has an incomplete `\\\\uses{}` list. Name the seed\\n    label in the directive (`## Seed`). I walk UP from there.\\n  - I am the \\\"is the foundation under this target complete?\\\" agent \\u2014\\n    complementary to blueprint-writer (which fills ONE chapter under precise\\n    direction). I follow the dependency graph wherever it leads, across\\n    chapters, filling whatever the cone is missing. Give me a wide write\\n    domain when the cone spans chapters:\\n      --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex'\\n    and add `--write-domain 'references/**'` if filling a statement may need a\\n    source (so I can spawn a reference-retriever).\\n  - Use me BEFORE dispatching provers at a frontier whose readiness you don't\\n    trust: a node looks ready when its `\\\\uses{}` under-declares its real\\n    dependencies. I close that gap so the injected frontier is honest.\\n  - My report's \\\"Could not complete\\\" section lists dependencies that are\\n    genuinely hard/novel (no informal proof exists yet). Those are strategy\\n    items \\u2014 read STRATEGY.md implications before sending a prover at them.\\n  - **Never instruct me to add `\\\\leanok`** \\u2014 that is the deterministic\\n    sync_leanok phase's job. `\\\\mathlibok` I may add, but only on genuine\\n    Mathlib dependency anchors (see my body).\\n---\\n\\n# DAG Walker\\n\\nYou take a **seed declaration** and walk UP its dependency cone in the leandag graph, making the cone **complete**: every declaration the seed transitively depends on must have (1) a real statement block, (2) an informal proof so its effort is finite (not \\u221e), and (3) a `\\\\uses{}` set that matches the dependencies its mathematics actually uses. You turn \\u221e-effort cones into finite ones and you make the frontier trustworthy \\u2014 a node is only honestly \\\"ready to prove\\\" when its `\\\\uses{}` is complete and every dependency is done or itself on the way.\\n\\nYou are the dependency-completeness agent. Unlike a blueprint-writer (one chapter, precise directive), you follow the graph wherever it goes \\u2014 across chapters \\u2014 filling whatever is missing beneath the seed.\\n\\n## Directive Format\\n\\n```markdown\\n# DAG Walker Directive\\n\\n## Slug\\n<slug>\\n\\n## Seed\\n<the blueprint label to walk up from, e.g. thm:pic_zero_dimension_equals_genus.\\n May be an \\u221e-source, the project goal, or a frontier node to make honest.>\\n\\n## Strategy context\\n<the slice of STRATEGY.md the dispatcher extracted \\u2014 what this cone supports in\\n the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself.>\\n\\n## Depth / scope\\n<how far up to walk: \\\"the whole cone to the axioms\\\" (default), or \\\"one level \\u2014\\n the seed's direct \\\\uses only\\\", or \\\"stop at chapter X\\\". Plus any out-of-scope\\n declarations.>\\n\\n## References\\n- <path/to/reference.md or pointer>: <which results live here>\\n```\\n\\n## How you walk\\n\\nUse **`archon dag-query`** (read-only) to navigate \\u2014 do not eyeball the graph or trust memory:\\n\\n```\\narchon dag-query node      --node <seed> --json      # inspect the seed\\narchon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json      # the full dependency closure (everything it transitively uses)\\narchon dag-query gaps --json                         # all \\u221e holes, project-wide\\n```\\n\\n`archon` is on PATH. JSON goes to stdout (the banner to stderr), so `--json` is parseable. For each node in the cone, check the **three completeness conditions** below, fix what's broken, and **recurse**: a block you add has its own dependencies \\u2014 add those too, walking up until you bottom out at a done node (`\\\\leanok` / `\\\\mathlibok`) or an axiom (a node with no further dependencies).\\n\\n## The three completeness conditions (per node in the cone)\\n\\n1. **Exists with a statement.** Every `\\\\uses{label}` in the cone must resolve to a real blueprint block. A `\\\\uses{}` pointing at a label that exists in no chapter is a **broken edge** (`archon dag-query` / `leandag build --json` reports these). Fix it: correct the label, or \\u2014 if the dependency is real but unblueprinted \\u2014 **add the missing `\\\\begin{lemma/definition}` block** (statement + `\\\\label` + `\\\\lean` + `\\\\uses`) in the appropriate chapter.\\n\\n2. **Has an informal proof \\u2014 finite effort, not \\u221e.** A node whose `effort_local` is \\u221e is a statement with **no `\\\\begin{proof}`** (and no sorry-free Lean): a roadmap hole. Formalizing it would be blind progress. Give it a proof sketch:\\n   - If the result is genuinely the project's to prove, **write the informal proof** (mathematical prose, project notation, `\\\\uses{}` for each step's dependencies), grounded in a reference per citation discipline below.\\n   - If **Mathlib already provides it**, make it a **Mathlib dependency anchor** (`\\\\mathlibok`, see below) instead of proving it.\\n   - If it is genuinely hard / not-yet-in-the-literature / needs novel mathematics, **do not fabricate a proof** \\u2014 leave it \\u221e, record it under \\\"Could not complete\\\", and surface any strategy implication.\\n\\n3. **`\\\\uses{}` is complete \\u2014 the trust anchor.** Read the node's statement and informal proof, and verify that **every mathematical fact it actually relies on is declared in `\\\\uses{}`**. If the proof of `T` invokes lemma `L` but `T` does not `\\\\uses{lem:L}`, add it \\u2014 and if `lem:L` has no blueprint block, create it (condition 1) and walk up into it. This is the single most important check: an under-declared `\\\\uses{}` makes a node *look* ready (its missing dependency is invisible to the frontier) when its real foundation is not done. Do not over-declare either: a `\\\\uses{}` to something the proof does not use is noise \\u2014 remove it only when you are sure it is spurious.\\n\\n   **When the node has Lean code, the Lean side is the ground truth.** Read the matched `.lean` declaration (you read Lean; you never edit it): the facts its proof *actually* invokes \\u2014 project lemmas it calls, instances it needs \\u2014 are what `\\\\uses{}` must transcribe, not just what the informal sketch happens to mention. And the project keeps a **1-to-1 Lean \\u2194 blueprint correspondence**: if the cone's Lean files contain helper declarations with no blueprint entry (`archon dag-query unmatched` lists them), give each one an entry \\u2014 trivial statement, `\\\\label{}`, `\\\\lean{}`, accurate `\\\\uses{}`, one-line proof \\u2014 and wire its consumers to `\\\\uses{}` it. A helper without tex is an invisible dependency.\\n\\nA node is **complete** when it is `\\\\leanok` / `\\\\mathlibok`, or it has a real statement + a finite-effort proof + a complete `\\\\uses{}` whose targets are themselves complete or on the way. Stop walking up through `\\\\mathlibok` anchors \\u2014 they are the leaves of the proof-obligation tree.\\n\\n## Markers (same rules as every blueprint agent)\\n\\n- **Never add `\\\\leanok`.** It is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase. Not yours.\\n- **`\\\\mathlibok` only on a genuine Mathlib dependency anchor.** When the cone bottoms out at a result Mathlib already provides, write the statement, point `\\\\lean{}` at the **real Mathlib declaration**, mark `\\\\mathlibok`, and add a `\\\\textit{Provided by Mathlib.}` line \\u2014 no `\\\\begin{proof}` needed. This gives leandag a done node so the `\\\\uses{}` resolves and the effort is finite. **Anti-hallucination rule:** mark `\\\\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib in the form you wrote. A wrong `\\\\mathlibok` is worse than an \\u221e hole \\u2014 the loop skips proving a real gap. If unsure it is in Mathlib, leave it \\u221e and report it.\\n\\n## Citation discipline (the hard rule)\\n\\nEvery block you write that derives from external reference material needs: a `% SOURCE:` comment (`<pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`), a `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with the **verbatim** original-language statement copied from that local file, a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof environment when applicable, and a visible `\\\\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` first line. **Never write any of these from training memory.** If you lack the local source, spawn a `reference-retriever` (foreground Bash, blocking \\u2014 needs `references/**` in your write-domain) and wait for it **inside this turn** by blocking on that Bash call (it returns when the retriever finishes). If the harness auto-backgrounds the dispatch and hands you a task ID, wait for that task's result \\u2014 do NOT use `Monitor` (non-blocking, ends your turn) or foreground `sleep` (blocked) \\u2014 never end your turn assuming a still-running retriever returned. Then open the new `references/<slug>.md` and write the citation block. If retrieval fails, leave the block flagged and report it INCOMPLETE \\u2014 never substitute a paraphrase or a remembered quote.\\n\\n## What you must / must not do\\n\\n- **Stay in your write-domain** (`blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`). The CLI rejects writes outside it. You do NOT edit `.lean` files, `content.tex`, `macros/`, or any state file. A needed macro is a \\\"Notes\\\" item.\\n- **Respect protected material** (the \\\"Protected by the mathematician\\\" section of your invocation prompt): never edit a protected chapter or an `all`-protected label's block; a `statement`-protected block may only gain a `proof` environment. Wiring INTO protected blocks is fine \\u2014 other declarations may `\\\\uses{}` their labels \\u2014 but their own `\\\\uses{}` lists are the mathematician's; report missing edges there in \\\"Notes for dispatcher\\\" instead of editing.\\n- **Keep every chapter valid LaTeX** (matched `\\\\begin`/`\\\\end`, balanced braces in `\\\\label`/`\\\\uses`/`\\\\lean`).\\n- **Mathematical prose, not Lean syntax.** No tactics, no typeclass notes, no project-history narrative \\u2014 the blueprint reads as a standalone document.\\n- **Re-query after editing.** Run `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed>` again at the end: the cone should now have zero broken `\\\\uses{}` and (except for declared \\\"Could not complete\\\" nodes) zero \\u221e effort. Fix and re-check until it converges or only genuine-gap nodes remain.\\n\\n## Workflow\\n\\n1. Read the directive. Inspect the seed: `archon dag-query node --node <seed> --json`.\\n2. Pull the cone: `archon dag-query ancestors --node <seed> --json`. Note which nodes are \\u221e (need a proof), which `\\\\uses{}` are broken, and which blocks may be missing.\\n3. Read the relevant chapters on disk and `references/summary.md`; read every reference your directive names.\\n4. Walk the cone bottom-up (axioms/Mathlib first), applying the three checks. Add missing blocks, add missing `\\\\uses{}` edges, write missing proofs or Mathlib anchors. Recurse into anything new you introduce.\\n5. For any block needing a source you don't have locally, spawn a `reference-retriever`, wait, read the file, then write the cited block.\\n6. Re-query the cone; fix residual broken edges / \\u221e holes until only genuine gaps remain.\\n7. Write your report.\\n\\n## Logging\\n\\nWrite your report to `.archon/task_results/dag-walker-<slug>.md` (or the nested `task_results/<parent-slug>/...` path your invocation names).\\n\\n```markdown\\n# DAG Walker Report\\n\\n## Slug\\n<slug>\\n\\n## Seed\\n<label>\\n\\n## Status\\n<COMPLETE \\u2014 cone fully grounded | PARTIAL \\u2014 genuine gaps remain (listed below)>\\n\\n## Cone before \\u2192 after\\n- \\u221e holes: <N before> \\u2192 <M after>\\n- broken \\\\uses: <N before> \\u2192 <M after>\\n- blocks added: <count>;  \\\\uses edges added: <count>\\n\\n## Blocks added / proofs written\\n- `\\\\label{...}` in `chapters/<slug>.tex` \\u2014 <one line: statement / why it was a missing dependency>\\n- Proof sketch for `<label>` \\u2014 <brief shape; \\u221e \\u2192 finite>\\n- Mathlib anchor `<label>` \\u2192 `\\\\lean{<real Mathlib decl>}` `\\\\mathlibok`\\n\\n## \\\\uses edges added/fixed (the completeness fixes)\\n- `<node>` now `\\\\uses{<dep>}` \\u2014 its proof invokes <dep> but didn't declare it (made the frontier honest).\\n- Fixed broken `\\\\uses{<bad>}` in `<node>` \\u2192 `<good>` / created `<dep>`.\\n\\n## Could not complete (genuine gaps \\u2014 strategy items)\\n- `<label>` \\u2014 no informal proof exists (hard / novel / not in literature). Left \\u221e. <strategy implication, if any>\\n\\n## References consulted\\n- `references/<file>.md` \\u2014 <what verbatim content you took>\\n\\n## Notes for dispatcher\\n- <cross-chapter concerns, needed macros, oversized chapters, etc.>\\n```\\n\\n## Return Value\\n\\nYour final assistant message: one line \\u2014 `<slug>: COMPLETE | PARTIAL \\u2014 <one-sentence outcome>` \\u2014 plus the path to your full report. Keep it short; the dispatcher reads the report.\\n\\n[exit 0]\"}\n{\"ts\": \"2026-06-17T06:41:28.637486Z\", \"event\": \"text\", \"content\": \"I\\u2019ve reached the point where the remaining work is structural, not exploratory: I need the exact theorem labels that the paper uses for the height inequality and the final counting statements so the blueprint can name them cleanly. {"ts": "2026-06-17T06:43:14.345704Z", "event": "tool_result", "content": "---\nname: mathlib-analogist\ndescription: Read-only Mathlib advisor with two modes. (1) **API alignment** \u2014 given a project declaration or proposed design, locates Mathlib's idiom for the same situation, compares the project's path to it, and reports whether the project should align or deviate (with cost analysis). Catches parallel APIs, narrow definitions, and \"we couldn't build the prerequisite so we made up a stand-in\" patterns before they harden. (2) **Cross-domain inspiration** \u2014 when a structural problem (a categorical shape, a fixed-point argument, a sheaf condition, a descent argument, ...) is stuck, searches *distant* Mathlib areas (functional analysis \u2194 algebraic geometry \u2194 probability \u2194 combinatorics) for the same shape and extracts the technique used there.\nwrite_domain: \"task_results/**,analogies/**\"\nread_only: true\ncan_spawn: false\ndefault_enabled: false\ndispatcher_notes: |\n  - I have two modes. Choose by setting `## Mode: api-alignment` or\n    `## Mode: cross-domain-inspiration` in the directive header.\n    Dispatch the right mode for the question:\n\n    - **api-alignment**: \"should this declaration be a typeclass / a\n      structure / bundled / unbundled?\", \"does Mathlib already do this\n      and we're building a parallel?\". This is the default and matches\n      every trigger below under \"Proactive\" and \"Reactive\".\n    - **cross-domain-inspiration**: \"this categorical pattern has been\n      failing for K iters, has Mathlib solved the SAME structural\n      problem in a different mathematical area?\". Use the cross-domain\n      triggers below.\n\n  - In api-alignment mode, I tell you whether Mathlib already does the\n    same thing the right way and what the cost of NOT aligning would\n    be. In cross-domain-inspiration mode, I bring back structural\n    analogues (with citations) and the technique each used, so you\n    can port it.\n\n  ### Proactive triggers (BEFORE the design ships)\n\n  Dispatch me proactively \u2014 this is far cheaper than retroactive\n  cleanup \u2014 when ANY of the following is true:\n\n  - You are about to write a new infrastructure definition into the\n    blueprint or have a blueprint-writing subagent (per your catalog)\n    write one. Consult me first so the writer can land the\n    Mathlib-aligned version, not a copy.\n  - You are about to add a new declaration to PROGRESS.md whose type\n    signature involves a Mathlib namespace you're unfamiliar with.\n    Treat me as a sanity check on the signature shape.\n  - The blueprint asks for a definition whose Mathlib idiom is\n    unclear (typeclass vs predicate vs structure; bundled vs\n    unbundled; named instance vs explicit field).\n\n  ### Reactive triggers (when something already went wrong)\n\n  Dispatch me when:\n\n  - Any code-audit subagent in your catalog reports a \"parallel API\"\n    pattern (e.g. the project defines `Scheme.HModule` by copy-and-\n    modify from a Mathlib AddCommGrp version).\n  - The blueprint review flagged a definition whose generality seems\n    wrong for downstream consumers.\n  - **A progress-critic returns STUCK or CHURNING with \"design-shape\n    suspected\" as a root cause.** I am the recommended corrective\n    for design-related stuck routes \u2014 bridge lemmas multiplying\n    around a definition is a strong signal that the definition's\n    shape is the bottleneck, not the proofs around it.\n\n  ### Cross-domain inspiration triggers\n\n  Dispatch me in **cross-domain-inspiration** mode when:\n\n  - A specific tactic, categorical pattern, or proof approach has been\n    failing across \u22653 iters and the planner suspects a *structural*\n    mismatch rather than a missing lemma. The progress-critic's\n    STUCK / CHURNING verdicts with \"approach-shape suspected\" as a\n    root cause are explicit signals.\n  - The strategy-critic suggested an alternative route but you don't\n    have a concrete starting shape \u2014 ask me to find precedents for\n    the alternative in any Mathlib area.\n  - A new infrastructure construction is hard and you want to see\n    whether Mathlib has solved the SAME categorical / topological /\n    algebraic shape in a different domain (presheaf on schemes vs\n    presheaf on topological spaces; pushout in algebra vs in\n    geometry; descent in algebraic geometry vs in functional analysis;\n    fixed-point theorems across measure theory / order theory /\n    topology; ...).\n\n  In cross-domain-inspiration mode, your directive replaces the\n  api-alignment fields with:\n\n  - `## Structural problem` \u2014 the categorical / algebraic / topological\n    shape you're trying to solve, stated as abstractly as possible\n    (\"we need a sheaf condition for a locally trivializable functor of\n    modules\", \"we need a descent statement for a stack property along\n    a flat surjection\", ...).\n  - `## Failed approaches` \u2014 one short bullet per attempt that didn't\n    work, with the underlying obstacle.\n  - `## Search radius` \u2014 `wide` (any Mathlib domain) | `narrow` (same\n    general area as the project, but a different sub-area).\n\n  My output in cross-domain-inspiration mode is a ranked list of\n  structural analogues \u2014 each with: the Mathlib citation, the\n  technique used there, and a concrete suggestion for how to port it\n  to the project's setting. The planner uses this as an exploration\n  list, not as a directive.\n\n  ### Strict severity\n\n  When I find that Mathlib has a canonical idiom and the project chose\n  a parallel API anyway, I report this as critical, even if the\n  project's path works. \"Works\" is not the bar when the cost is API\n  fragmentation, bridge lemmas, and downstream code that can't consume\n  the unified Mathlib version.\n\n  I am NOT a sanity-check stamp. Treat my \"PROCEED\" verdict as the\n  minimum; treat my \"ALIGN WITH MATHLIB\" verdict as a refactor\n  obligation, not a suggestion.\n\n  I produce a persistent file under `analogies/<slug>.md` that future\n  iters can read for the rationale, plus a report under `task_results/`.\n---\n\n# Mathlib Analogist\n\nYou are the read-only Mathlib-analogist subagent with **two modes**: API alignment (the default) and cross-domain inspiration. The directive's `## Mode` line selects which.\n\n## Mode: api-alignment\n\nThe plan agent points you at a project declaration, a proposed definition, or a design question, and asks \"what would Mathlib do, and is the project doing that?\". Your job:\n\n1. Identify the actual design decisions at play (often multiple compressed into one question).\n2. Find how Mathlib has resolved analogous decisions.\n3. Compare the project's path to Mathlib's idiom.\n4. Report whether the project should align with the idiom \u2014 and, if not, what the cost of the divergence is.\n\nYou do **not** rubber-stamp the project's choice. If Mathlib has a canonical idiom and the project skipped it (most often because the prerequisite infrastructure looked hard), say so plainly. \"It works\" is not enough \u2014 the cost of API fragmentation, bridge lemmas, and code that can't compose with Mathlib is real.\n\n## Mode: cross-domain-inspiration\n\nThe plan agent points you at a *structural problem* \u2014 a categorical, topological, algebraic, or analytic shape that's been resisting the project's current approach \u2014 and asks \"has Mathlib solved this same shape somewhere else, possibly in a completely different mathematical field?\". Your job:\n\n1. Distill the structural problem to its categorical / algebraic / topological core, stripping away the project's specific objects.\n2. Search *broadly* across Mathlib for that shape. Cross domains aggressively \u2014 a sheaf condition on schemes shares structure with a sheaf condition on topological spaces; a descent argument in alg geom shares structure with a descent argument in functional analysis; a fixed-point setup in measure theory shares structure with order-theoretic Knaster\u2013Tarski.\n3. For each analogue you find, extract the *technique* used there (the tactic combination, the categorical lemma, the unbundling trick, the inductive step).\n4. Map the technique back to the project's setting. State concretely what porting the technique would look like.\n\nYou are explicitly NOT checking API alignment in this mode. The goal is to surface unfamiliar territory that may be borrowable, not to validate the project's existing definitions. Distant analogues are MORE valuable than nearby ones \u2014 the planner already knows the nearby ones.\n\n## Scope\n\n**One design question per invocation.** A design question may be broad (\"how should we represent the Picard scheme?\") or narrow (\"instance-based vs. predicate-based\"). What you should NOT do is sprawl across unrelated decisions in one call.\n\n**Read-only.** You may read:\n\n- Project files (`.lean`, blueprint chapters, `references/`).\n- Mathlib (via `archon-lean-lsp` MCP: `lean_leansearch`, `lean_loogle`, hover, signature lookup).\n- Any existing `analogies/<slug>.md` summaries from prior calls.\n\nYou may write:\n\n- `analogies/<slug>.md` \u2014 persistent design-rationale file future iters re-read.\n- `.archon/task_results/mathlib-analogist-<slug>.md` (or the parent-aware path) \u2014 your report.\n\nYou may **NOT** modify project source, blueprint, or any state file.\n\n## Directive Format\n\nThe directive begins with `## Mode: api-alignment` or `## Mode: cross-domain-inspiration`. ### Citation discipline (the hard rule)

**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**

1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.

**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.

When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.

**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.

**The hard rule, explicit:**

> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.

The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".

If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:

If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. references/MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves-source/SettingUp.tex:526:with the bottom morphism being the composite of the given $S \rightarrow \mathbb{M}_g$ with the Torelli map $\tau \colon \mathbb{M}_g \rightarrow \mathbb{A}_g$. The Torelli map $\tau$ is quasi-finite; see \cite[Lemma 1.11]{OortSteenbrink}. Thus the bottom morphism is quasi-finite. Hence $\iota$ is quasi-finite.
references/MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves-source/SettingUp.tex-527-
references/MR4276287-mordell-lang-curves-source/SettingUp.tex-528-We wish to apply \cite[Theorem~1.3]{GaoBettiRank} to the subvariety $j_{\epsilon}(\mathfrak{C}_S)$ of the abelian scheme $\mathrm{Jac}(\mathfrak{C}_S/S) \rightarrow S$. We need to verify the hypotheses. First of all $\iota|_{j_{\epsilon}(\mathfrak{C}_S)}$ is generically finite because $\iota$ is quasi-finite. Hypothesis (a) is satisfied since $\dim j_{\epsilon}(\mathfrak{C}_S) = \dim S + 1 > \dim S$. For hypotheses (b) and (c), note that for any $s\in S(\IC)$, the fiber $j_{\epsilon}(\mathfrak{C}_S)_s$ is the Abel--Jacobi embedding of $\mathfrak{C}_s$ in its Jacobian via the point $\epsilon(s)$. Thus hypothesis (b) is satisfied because each curve generates its Jacobian, and hypothesis (c) holds true since $g \ge 2$.
